title: Devil's food cake
subtitle: An American dark chocolate layer cake with ganache frosting
excerpt: An American dark chocolate layer cake with a deeply flavoured crumb from cocoa and a little coffee. Frosted with a glossy dark ganache. Makes one two-layer 20 cm cake.
sourceNotes: Devil's food cake is an American chocolate layer cake tradition documented from the early twentieth century. The name appears in American household baking guides from around 1905 onwards, contrasted with the paler angel food cake. The defining features are consistent across those publications: a very dark, moist crumb from a higher proportion of cocoa than standard chocolate cake, and the use of boiling water or hot coffee to bloom the cocoa.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfrosted sponge layers. Wrap individually and freeze for up to 2 months. Frost from thawed.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps at room temperature for 2 days under a dome, or refrigerated for 4 days. Bring to room temperature before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 190°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: A mixture of melted chocolate and hot cream, emulsified together into a smooth, glossy sauce or frosting. The ratio of chocolate to cream determines the final texture: equal parts give a pourable sauce that sets to a soft frosting; two parts chocolate to one cream gives a firm ganache for truffles or a thick glaze. Stir from the centre outward as you add the cream to build a stable emulsion.

content.text: The difference between a devil's food cake and a standard chocolate cake is depth: more cocoa, hot liquid to bloom it, and a touch of buttermilk for lift and tenderness. The boiling water in the batter is not a mistake. It opens up the cocoa particles and releases more flavour than cold liquid would. The batter will look thin; bake it anyway.

Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Grease and line two 20 cm round tins. Whisk together the {{plain-flour}}, {{cocoa-powder}}, {{dark-brown-sugar}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. In a separate bowl, whisk the {{eggs}}, {{buttermilk}}, {{vegetable-oil}}, and {{vanilla-extract}}. Pour the wet into the dry and mix until smooth. Add 240 ml boiling water and stir until combined. The batter will be thin. Divide between the tins.

Bake for 28–32 minutes, until a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean and the layers have pulled away slightly from the sides of the tins. Cool in the tins for 10 minutes, then turn out and cool completely before frosting.

Place the chopped {{dark-chocolate}} in a heatproof bowl. Heat the {{double-cream}} in a small saucepan until just below a boil, then pour over the chocolate. Leave for 1 minute, then stir from the centre outward until a smooth

forms. Leave to thicken at room temperature for 30–45 minutes, until spreadable but not set. Spread half over one sponge layer, top with the second layer, and spread the remaining ganache over the top and sides.
